APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses an important efficiency problem in neural machine translation (predicting large target vocabularies) and proposes practical methods (binary code prediction, convolutional error-correcting codes, and a hybrid softmax/binary model) that are squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The evaluation uses standard NMT datasets (ASPEC, BTEC), BLEU scoring, and compares against appropriate baselines (softmax variants), with hyperparameters reported and claimed substantial memory and CPU decoding improvements — all of which target the conference audience interested in scalable, deployable NMT systems. From a reproducible-systems perspective the topic, empirical framing, and chosen baselines make it a strong fit for a top-tier venue. | B(5): This paper addresses a core scalability problem in neural MT (softmax over large vocabularies) with a conceptually novel and practically oriented solution (binary-code prediction, error-correcting codes, hybrid softmax/binary) that is squarely within the interests of top NLP/ML venues. The methodology and evaluation (ASPEC/BTEC, BLEU, comparisons to standard and hierarchical/differentiated softmax baselines) use community-standard practices, and the reported trade-offs (accuracy close to softmax with large memory and CPU gains) are highly relevant to both researchers and practitioners. The problem formulation, metrics, and related work coverage are appropriate for the venue, and the proposed techniques are likely to influence follow-up work on efficient decoding and vocabulary compression.

The evaluation uses standard NMT datasets (ASPEC, BTEC), BLEU scoring, and compares against appropriate baselines (softmax variants), with hyperparameters reported and claimed substantial memory and CPU decoding improvements — all of which target the conference audience interested in scalable, deployable NMT systems. From a reproducible-systems perspective the topic, empirical framing, and chosen baselines make it a strong fit for a top-tier venue.

The problem formulation, metrics, and related work coverage are appropriate for the venue, and the proposed techniques are likely to influence follow-up work on efficient decoding and vocabulary compression.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against an appropriate set of baselines (standard softmax, hierarchical softmax, differentiated softmax, and sampling-based approximations) on the same datasets (ASPEC, BTEC) using BLEU, which supports fair comparisons. It explicitly acknowledges limitations of the naive binary mapping and introduces controlled remedies (convolutional ECC + hybrid softmax) with analyses showing tradeoffs in accuracy, memory, and decoding speed. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and competing approaches are cited and discussed. From a reproducibility/benchmarking perspective the comparisons appear comprehensive and relevant to the claims. | B(5): The paper evaluates against a broad set of relevant baselines (standard softmax, hierarchical softmax, differentiated softmax, and sampling-based approximations) on the same datasets (ASPEC, BTEC) using BLEU, which supports fair comparisons. It reports hyperparameters and discusses limitations, and the related-work section is described as adequate, indicating the authors both position their contributions and acknowledge tradeoffs where prior methods can be competitive. From my perspective as an NMT researcher, the empirical setup and choice of baselines are comprehensive and appropriate for the claims made.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against an appropriate set of baselines (standard softmax, hierarchical softmax, differentiated softmax, and sampling-based approximations) on the same datasets (ASPEC, BTEC) using BLEU, which supports fair comparisons. From a reproducibility/benchmarking perspective the comparisons appear comprehensive and relevant to the claims.

It reports hyperparameters and discusses limitations, and the related-work section is described as adequate, indicating the authors both position their contributions and acknowledge tradeoffs where prior methods can be competitive. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and presents experiments on ASPEC and BTEC showing BLEU close to softmax while reducing memory and improving CPU decoding speed; the convolutional ECC + hybrid design addresses the robustness issues of the naive binary mapping. However, from a reproducibility standpoint important details are missing or unclear in the summary (no code mentioned, limited reporting of run-to-run variability, hardware/time breakdowns, seeds and full memory/latency breakdowns), and statistical rigor (multiple runs/significance) appears absent. These are minor but important gaps that do not invalidate the technical approach or main conclusions, but do limit confidence in the generality and reproducibility of the reported gains. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical results showing large reductions in output-layer memory and notable CPU decoding speedups while approaching softmax BLEU scores, and the use of convolutional error-correcting codes plus a hybrid softmax/binary scheme is a plausible solution to the robustness problem. There are no complex mathematical proofs required and the described methods are internally consistent; however the summary does not indicate formal analyses of error bounds for the coding scheme or statistical significance testing for BLEU gains, which would strengthen the evidence. Experimental methodology appears sound in terms of baselines (standard and variant softmax approaches) and datasets (ASPEC, BTEC), but lack of reported statistical tests and absent details on some controls are minor concerns. Overall the technical claims are supported by the presented experiments, with only modest missing evidence that does not overturn the conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and presents experiments on ASPEC and BTEC showing BLEU close to softmax while reducing memory and improving CPU decoding speed; the convolutional ECC + hybrid design addresses the robustness issues of the naive binary mapping. However, from a reproducibility standpoint important details are missing or unclear in the summary (no code mentioned, limited reporting of run-to-run variability, hardware/time breakdowns, seeds and full memory/latency breakdowns), and statistical rigor (multiple runs/significance) appears absent. These are minor but important gaps that do not invalidate the technical approach or main conclusions, but do limit confidence in the generality and reproducibility of the reported gains.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical results showing large reductions in output-layer memory and notable CPU decoding speedups while approaching softmax BLEU scores, and the use of convolutional error-correcting codes plus a hybrid softmax/binary scheme is a plausible solution to the robustness problem. There are no complex mathematical proofs required and the described methods are internally consistent; however the summary does not indicate formal analyses of error bounds for the coding scheme or statistical significance testing for BLEU gains, which would strengthen the evidence. Experimental methodology appears sound in terms of baselines (standard and variant softmax approaches) and datasets (ASPEC, BTEC), but lack of reported statistical tests and absent details on some controls are minor concerns. Overall the technical claims are supported by the presented experiments, with only modest missing evidence that does not overturn the conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a distinctive approach—predicting compact binary codes per target word and using convolutional error-correcting codes with Viterbi decoding plus a hybrid softmax/binary scheme—that is not a standard treatment in NMT output-layer design and is clearly differentiated from hierarchical/differentiated/sampling softmaxes. The use of ECCs and Viterbi decoding to improve robustness of bit-wise prediction is a non-trivial and practical extension beyond simply mapping words to bit vectors. From my reproducible-systems perspective the idea is notable and would inform experts evaluating memory/latency trade-offs, though it builds on known concepts (binary coding, ECCs, hybrid prediction) rather than introducing a wholly new theoretical paradigm. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly new engineering direction—predicting compact binary codes for target words and decoding them with convolutional error-correcting codes and Viterbi—rather than relying on standard softmax or hierarchical decompositions. Combining error-correcting codes with a hybrid softmax/binary predictor to trade off robustness and efficiency is a non-trivial and practically motivated extension beyond prior vocabulary-compression or hierarchical methods. The approach is distinguishable from existing softmax approximations and would likely surprise and inform experts about a different space of accuracy/efficiency trade-offs, though it builds on known coding and hybridization ideas rather than wholly new theory.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a distinctive approach—predicting compact binary codes per target word and using convolutional error-correcting codes with Viterbi decoding plus a hybrid softmax/binary scheme—that is not a standard treatment in NMT output-layer design and is clearly differentiated from hierarchical/differentiated/sampling softmaxes. The use of ECCs and Viterbi decoding to improve robustness of bit-wise prediction is a non-trivial and practical extension beyond simply mapping words to bit vectors. From my reproducible-systems perspective the idea is notable and would inform experts evaluating memory/latency trade-offs, though it builds on known concepts (binary coding, ECCs, hybrid prediction) rather than introducing a wholly new theoretical paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new engineering direction—predicting compact binary codes for target words and decoding them with convolutional error-correcting codes and Viterbi—rather than relying on standard softmax or hierarchical decompositions. Combining error-correcting codes with a hybrid softmax/binary predictor to trade off robustness and efficiency is a non-trivial and practically motivated extension beyond prior vocabulary-compression or hierarchical methods. The approach is distinguishable from existing softmax approximations and would likely surprise and inform experts about a different space of accuracy/efficiency trade-offs, though it builds on known coding and hybridization ideas rather than wholly new theory.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles an important and practical bottleneck—softmax output cost for large vocabularies—and presents a compelling alternative (binary-code prediction with ECC and a hybrid softmax) that yields substantial memory reductions (≈1/10–1/1000) and CPU decoding speedups (≈5–20×) while retaining BLEU close to standard softmax on ASPEC and BTEC. From a reproducibility/engineering perspective the reported hyperparameters and clear design (convolutional ECC + Viterbi decoding, hybrid pathway) make it a viable approach for practitioners concerned with model size and inference latency. While evaluation on two NMT datasets limits certainty about generality, the technique is likely to influence work on efficient output layers across NLP and open useful follow-ups (larger-scale validation, integration into production stacks). | B(4): This paper tackles an important practical bottleneck in NMT—the expensive softmax over very large vocabularies—and proposes a concrete alternative (binary code prediction with convolutional error-correcting codes and a hybrid softmax/binary model) that yields substantial memory reductions (10x–1000x) and CPU decoding speedups (≈5x–20x). The hybrid and ECC components make the approach credible and nearly match softmax accuracy on ASPEC and BTEC, so practitioners seeking lightweight or CPU-friendly decoders will likely consider and build on this. While the evaluation is limited to two datasets and some robustness questions remain, the idea opens promising directions for scalable vocabularies and efficient deployment across languages and devices, so it should influence follow-up work and engineering adoption.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles an important and practical bottleneck—softmax output cost for large vocabularies—and presents a compelling alternative (binary-code prediction with ECC and a hybrid softmax) that yields substantial memory reductions (≈1/10–1/1000) and CPU decoding speedups (≈5–20×) while retaining BLEU close to standard softmax on ASPEC and BTEC. From a reproducibility/engineering perspective the reported hyperparameters and clear design (convolutional ECC + Viterbi decoding, hybrid pathway) make it a viable approach for practitioners concerned with model size and inference latency. While evaluation on two NMT datasets limits certainty about generality, the technique is likely to influence work on efficient output layers across NLP and open useful follow-ups (larger-scale validation, integration into production stacks).

The hybrid and ECC components make the approach credible and nearly match softmax accuracy on ASPEC and BTEC, so practitioners seeking lightweight or CPU-friendly decoders will likely consider and build on this. While the evaluation is limited to two datasets and some robustness questions remain, the idea opens promising directions for scalable vocabularies and efficient deployment across languages and devices, so it should influence follow-up work and engineering adoption.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion; the main contributions (binary code prediction, ECC+Viterbi, hybrid softmax) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, which supports reproducibility from a presentation standpoint. Figures, tables, and examples are described as effective and informative, and the writing quality is generally good. I deduct one point because a few methodological choices (e.g., the exact mapping and robustness discussions) could be explained more explicitly for readers trying to implement the approach from the text alone. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (binary-code output layer, convolutional error-correcting codes with Viterbi decoding, and a hybrid softmax/binary approach) are stated early and carried through method and experiments. Notation appears consistent and many practical details (datasets ASPEC/BTEC, hyperparameters, baselines, BLEU results) are reported, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples are described as effective, and overall writing quality is good, though a few sections (e.g., the initial simple mapping failure modes and the transition to error-correction/hybrid solutions) could be clearer about intuition for readers less familiar with coding theory. From a reader-in-practice perspective the presentation is strong with only minor clarity issues.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion; the main contributions (binary code prediction, ECC+Viterbi, hybrid softmax) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, which supports reproducibility from a presentation standpoint. Figures, tables, and examples are described as effective and informative, and the writing quality is generally good. I deduct one point because a few methodological choices (e.g., the exact mapping and robustness discussions) could be explained more explicitly for readers trying to implement the approach from the text alone.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (binary-code output layer, convolutional error-correcting codes with Viterbi decoding, and a hybrid softmax/binary approach) are stated early and carried through method and experiments. Notation appears consistent and many practical details (datasets ASPEC/BTEC, hyperparameters, baselines, BLEU results) are reported, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples are described as effective, and overall writing quality is good, though a few sections (e.g., the initial simple mapping failure modes and the transition to error-correction/hybrid solutions) could be clearer about intuition for readers less familiar with coding theory. From a reader-in-practice perspective the presentation is strong with only minor clarity issues.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper evaluates on two NMT datasets (ASPEC, BTEC) and compares against several softmax baselines while reporting BLEU, and it introduces ECC and a hybrid softmax/binary approach to address robustness — so the core claims are tested. However, from the summary it is unclear whether systematic ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying bit-length, ECC parameters, hybrid cutoff) and multiple runs/statistical significance were provided; hardware, wall-clock training/decoding details and code release are also not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility of the claimed 5x–20x CPU speedups and memory breakdowns. Related work appears adequately covered and limitations are acknowledged, but the experimental rigor needed for production confidence (detailed latency/memory profiles, seeds, and comprehensive ablations) is missing. | B(4): The experiments compare against multiple baselines (standard softmax, hierarchical/differentiated softmax, sampling approximations) on two NMT datasets (ASPEC, BTEC) and report BLEU, showing substantial memory and CPU gains, which supports the main claims. The paper also studies robustness mitigations (convolutional error-correcting codes with Viterbi decoding and a hybrid softmax/binary model), effectively providing useful ablation/sensitivity analysis of the proposed components. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and limitations (degraded quality for naive mapping and need for additional techniques) are discussed, but evaluation is limited to two datasets and there is no mention of public code or broader language/beam-search analyses, leaving some reproducibility and generalization questions open.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates on two NMT datasets (ASPEC, BTEC) and compares against several softmax baselines while reporting BLEU, and it introduces ECC and a hybrid softmax/binary approach to address robustness — so the core claims are tested. However, from the summary it is unclear whether systematic ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying bit-length, ECC parameters, hybrid cutoff) and multiple runs/statistical significance were provided; hardware, wall-clock training/decoding details and code release are also not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility of the claimed 5x–20x CPU speedups and memory breakdowns. Related work appears adequately covered and limitations are acknowledged, but the experimental rigor needed for production confidence (detailed latency/memory profiles, seeds, and comprehensive ablations) is missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments compare against multiple baselines (standard softmax, hierarchical/differentiated softmax, sampling approximations) on two NMT datasets (ASPEC, BTEC) and report BLEU, showing substantial memory and CPU gains, which supports the main claims. The paper also studies robustness mitigations (convolutional error-correcting codes with Viterbi decoding and a hybrid softmax/binary model), effectively providing useful ablation/sensitivity analysis of the proposed components. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and limitations (degraded quality for naive mapping and need for additional techniques) are discussed, but evaluation is limited to two datasets and there is no mention of public code or broader language/beam-search analyses, leaving some reproducibility and generalization questions open.

main_claims: Proposes predicting a binary code for each target word to compute the NMT output layer more efficiently, reducing computation to be logarithmic in vocabulary size in the best case.
Introduces use of convolutional error-correcting codes (with Viterbi decoding) and a hybrid softmax/binary approach to improve robustness and quality of binary code prediction.

Demonstrates that the proposed model achieves translation accuracies approaching standard softmax while reducing memory usage by roughly 1/10 to 1/1000 and improving CPU decoding speed by about 5x to 20x.
_summary.methodology: Replace standard one-hot softmax word prediction with a binary-code prediction for each word (B bits), combine with convolutional error-correcting codes and Viterbi decoding for robustness, and optionally use a hybrid model that predicts frequent words via softmax and others via binary codes.
limitations: The simplest direct mapping of words to bit arrays seriously decreases translation quality (robustness issues), requiring additional techniques (error-correcting codes, hybrid model).

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Systems Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in machine learning focusing on reproducible deep-learning systems and production deployments of NLP models. Has led implementation and benchmarking efforts for several open-source NMT toolkits and participated in industrial evaluations of model-speed/size tradeoffs.
traits: Insists on clear, detailed experimental setups (hyperparameters, random seeds, hardware, and training time) to enable reproduction.
Prioritizes thorough baselines and ablations that isolate the contribution of each component (e.g., binary coding, ECC, hybrid softmax).
Checks statistical rigor: reports of variability, significance or multiple runs when improvements are small.
Values practical considerations: memory breakdowns, wall-clock decoding latency on CPU/GPU, and implementation complexity.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments and presented results convincingly support the claims and whether the method can be reliably reproduced and deployed.

reviewer_b.name: NMT Researcher & Applications Lead
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in neural machine translation with a track record of publications on efficient decoding, vocabulary handling, and model compression, and experience advising deployment of MT in real-world products across many languages.
traits: Assesses novelty and positioning relative to prior work on vocabulary compression, hierarchical/target selection, and binary/continuous label representations.
Evaluates broader impact: applicability across languages, scaling behavior, and whether tradeoffs (accuracy vs. efficiency) are compelling for real use-cases.
Looks for conceptual clarity: whether the proposed coding/decoding approach is theoretically motivated and how it generalizes.
Pays attention to limitations and failure modes (e.g., low-resource languages, rare words, beam search interaction).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper by how it advances the field conceptually and practically, and whether its contributions will influence future research or adoption in real MT systems.
